model_narrative: This is a static planning model for a national fertilizer sector. We decide how intensely to run each production process at each plant, how much of each final product to ship from plants to demand regions, how much of each shippable intermediate to move between plants, how much of each final product and each raw material to import, and how much of each raw material to buy domestically at each plant. Several cost components are tracked separately: a domestic recurrent cost, a transport cost, and an import cost. The objective is to minimize the discounted total cost of meeting the country's fertilizer demand.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the plant locations where production and processing can take place

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the demand regions that consume final fertilizer products

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the productive units, the physical equipment whose installed capacity limits production

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the production processes that can be operated at a plant

sets.name: cq
sets.doc: the plant nutrients, nitrogen and phosphate

sets.name: c
sets.doc: all commodities in the model: final products, intermediates, and raw/utility inputs

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final products sold to demand regions; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: ci
sets.doc: the intermediate products produced and consumed inside the sector; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: cs
sets.doc: the intermediates that may be shipped between plants; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: cr
sets.doc: the domestic raw materials and miscellaneous inputs; a subset of the commodities

params.name: cf75
params.doc: the 1974-75 fertilizer requirement of each demand region for each final product, in thousand tons per year; a region requires a product only where this is positive

params.name: a
params.doc: input-output coefficients per unit of process level: a positive value means the process produces that commodity, a negative value means it consumes that commodity, zero means no involvement

params.name: bcap
params.doc: capacity-utilization coefficients giving how much of a productive unit's capacity one unit of a process consumes

params.name: pv
params.doc: import prices, cif US dollars per ton 1975; a commodity is importable only where this is positive

params.name: pd
params.doc: domestic purchase prices of raw materials at each plant; a raw material can be purchased domestically at a plant only where this is positive

params.name: k
params.doc: the installed capacity of each productive unit at each plant, in thousand tons per year; a unit exists at a plant only where this is positive

params.name: muf
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of shipping final products from a plant to a demand region, in pounds per ton

params.name: mufv
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of delivering imported final products from the import point to a demand region

params.name: mui
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of shipping intermediates between two plants

params.name: mur
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of moving imported raw materials inland to a plant

params.name: er
params.doc: the exchange rate converting foreign-currency import value into local pounds

params.name: util
params.doc: the maximum capacity utilization fraction allowed on any productive unit

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the operating level of each process at each plant, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: xf
vars.doc: domestic shipment of each final product from each plant to each demand region, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: xi
vars.doc: domestic shipment of each shippable intermediate from one plant to another; in this scenario interplant intermediate shipment is turned off so these are all fixed to zero

vars.name: vf
vars.doc: imports of each final product delivered to each demand region, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: vr
vars.doc: imports of each raw material received at each plant, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: u
vars.doc: domestic purchases of each raw material at each plant, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: psi
vars.doc: the discounted total cost, the quantity minimized

vars.name: psip
vars.doc: the domestic recurrent cost component, in thousand pounds per year

vars.name: psil
vars.doc: the transport cost component, in thousand pounds per year

vars.name: psii
vars.doc: the import cost component, in thousand pounds per year

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Define the domestic recurrent cost. It equals the total amount spent buying raw materials domestically, summed over every plant and raw material that can be purchased domestically at that plant, valuing each purchase at that plant's local price for that material. Set the domestic recurrent cost equal to this total.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Define the transport cost. It collects four kinds of freight: shipping final products from the plants that make them out to the demand regions, delivering imported final products from the import point to the demand regions, moving shippable intermediates between plants, and hauling imported raw materials inland to the plants that receive them. Each movement is valued at its own per-ton transport rate. Set the transport cost equal to the sum of all these freight charges.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Define the import cost. The foreign value of imports is the total spent on imported final products delivered to the demand regions plus the total spent on imported raw materials received at the plants, each valued at its import price. Convert that foreign value into local currency through the exchange rate, and set the import cost equal to it.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Make sure every demand region's requirement for each final product it needs is met. For each region and each final product the region requires, the total of that product shipped in from the domestic plants that make it, plus any of it imported to the region when the product is importable, must be at least the region's requirement for that product.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Keep each commodity in balance at each plant, so that the plant never uses or ships out more of a commodity than it has available there. For each commodity and plant, net production by the plant's processes, plus shippable intermediates received from other plants minus those sent to other plants, plus imported and domestically purchased raw materials arriving at the plant, minus final product shipped out from the plant, must be nonnegative. Each term applies only where it is structurally possible for that commodity and plant.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Respect the installed capacity of every productive unit. For each productive unit that exists at a plant, the total capacity it consumes across all processes run at that plant must not exceed the allowed utilization fraction of that unit's installed capacity there.

constraints.name: psi
constraints.intent: Tie the total cost together. The discounted total cost equals the sum of the domestic recurrent cost, the transport cost, and the import cost.

query: There's a proposal on the table to firm up Aswan's role as the upper-Egypt CAN-310 hub: across the three southern markets it serves — sohag, quena and aswan town — the calcium-ammonium-nitrate it ships should add up to at least 230 between them. What does the optimizer return for total cost once Aswan's combined southern dispatch is held to that floor?

query: Engineering is pitching a uniform de-rating on the nitric-acid units for a safety retrofit: every plant that runs a nitric-acid line — both Aswan and Helwan — would be held to a process level of no more than 130 each. Plug that ceiling in across all the nitric-acid sites and walk me through where total cost lands.
expected_pyomo:
def _nitr_cap(model, i): return model.z['nitr-acid', i] <= 130.0
model.nitr_uniform_cap = Constraint(['aswan', 'helwan'], rule=_nitr_cap)

query: The board is pitching a self-sufficiency package for Aswan's calcium-ammonium-nitrate line, two moves bundled together: Aswan must keep at least 80 of its own can-310 to cover the aswan home market, and at the same time its total can-310 process level stays held at its current 235 ceiling — no expanding the line to absorb the extra local hold. Run that package and tell me the resulting total cost.

query: Our three single-superphosphate plants — assiout, kafr-el-zt and abu-zaabal — are collectively running flat out, and leaning that hard on domestic SSP makes me nervous about a single bad season. Couldn't we ease off and keep the combined SSP-155 process level across all three plants under 450 in total? What's the model seeing that makes it run the whole superphosphate fleet so hard?

vars.doc: the import cost component, in thousand pounds per year

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Define the domestic recurrent cost. It equals the total amount spent buying raw materials domestically, summed over every plant and raw material that can be purchased domestically at that plant, valuing each purchase at that plant's local price for that material. Set the domestic recurrent cost equal to this total.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Define the transport cost. It collects four kinds of freight: shipping final products from the plants that make them out to the demand regions, delivering imported final products from the import point to the demand regions, moving shippable intermediates between plants, and hauling imported raw materials inland to the plants that receive them. Each movement is valued at its own per-ton transport rate. Set the transport cost equal to the sum of all these freight charges.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Define the import cost. The foreign value of imports is the total spent on imported final products delivered to the demand regions plus the total spent on imported raw materials received at the plants, each valued at its import price. Convert that foreign value into local currency through the exchange rate, and set the import cost equal to it.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Make sure every demand region's requirement for each final product it needs is met. For each region and each final product the region requires, the total of that product shipped in from the domestic plants that make it, plus any of it imported to the region when the product is importable, must be at least the region's requirement for that product.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Keep each commodity in balance at each plant, so that the plant never uses or ships out more of a commodity than it has available there. For each commodity and plant, net production by the plant's processes, plus shippable intermediates received from other plants minus those sent to other plants, plus imported and domestically purchased raw materials arriving at the plant, minus final product shipped out from the plant, must be nonnegative. Each term applies only where it is structurally possible for that commodity and plant.

constraints.name: i);
constraints.intent: Respect the installed capacity of every productive unit. For each productive unit that exists at a plant, the total capacity it consumes across all processes run at that plant must not exceed the allowed utilization fraction of that unit's installed capacity there.

constraints.name: psi
constraints.intent: Tie the total cost together. The discounted total cost equals the sum of the domestic recurrent cost, the transport cost, and the import cost.

query: assiout sits closest to the upper-Egypt phosphate demand, yet the plan leaves it as the smallest of the three SSP-155 producers while kafr-el-zt and abu-zaabal carry more. Shouldn't assiout pull at least 40 percent of the combined superphosphate output across the three plants — its share measured against the sum of all three plants' SSP-155 process levels? What's pulling the model to underweight the plant nearest the demand?

model_narrative: This is a static planning model for a national fertilizer sector. We decide how intensely to run each production process at each plant, how much of each final product to ship from plants to demand regions, how much of each shippable intermediate to move between plants, how much of each final product and each raw material to import, and how much of each raw material to buy domestically at each plant. Several cost components are tracked separately: a domestic recurrent cost, a transport cost, and an import cost. The objective is to minimize the discounted total cost of meeting the country's fertilizer demand.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the plant locations where production and processing can take place

sets.name: j
sets.doc: the demand regions that consume final fertilizer products

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the productive units, the physical equipment whose installed capacity limits production

sets.name: p
sets.doc: the production processes that can be operated at a plant

sets.name: cq
sets.doc: the plant nutrients, nitrogen and phosphate

sets.name: c
sets.doc: all commodities in the model: final products, intermediates, and raw/utility inputs

sets.name: cf
sets.doc: the final products sold to demand regions; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: ci
sets.doc: the intermediate products produced and consumed inside the sector; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: cs
sets.doc: the intermediates that may be shipped between plants; a subset of the commodities

sets.name: cr
sets.doc: the domestic raw materials and miscellaneous inputs; a subset of the commodities

params.name: cf75
params.doc: the 1974-75 fertilizer requirement of each demand region for each final product, in thousand tons per year; a region requires a product only where this is positive

params.name: a
params.doc: input-output coefficients per unit of process level: a positive value means the process produces that commodity, a negative value means it consumes that commodity, zero means no involvement

params.name: bcap
params.doc: capacity-utilization coefficients giving how much of a productive unit's capacity one unit of a process consumes

params.name: pv
params.doc: import prices, cif US dollars per ton 1975; a commodity is importable only where this is positive

params.name: pd
params.doc: domestic purchase prices of raw materials at each plant; a raw material can be purchased domestically at a plant only where this is positive

params.name: k
params.doc: the installed capacity of each productive unit at each plant, in thousand tons per year; a unit exists at a plant only where this is positive

params.name: muf
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of shipping final products from a plant to a demand region, in pounds per ton

params.name: mufv
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of delivering imported final products from the import point to a demand region

params.name: mui
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of shipping intermediates between two plants

params.name: mur
params.doc: the per-ton transport cost of moving imported raw materials inland to a plant

params.name: er
params.doc: the exchange rate converting foreign-currency import value into local pounds

params.name: util
params.doc: the maximum capacity utilization fraction allowed on any productive unit

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the operating level of each process at each plant, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: xf
vars.doc: domestic shipment of each final product from each plant to each demand region, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: xi
vars.doc: domestic shipment of each shippable intermediate from one plant to another; in this scenario interplant intermediate shipment is turned off so these are all fixed to zero

vars.name: vf
vars.doc: imports of each final product delivered to each demand region, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: vr
vars.doc: imports of each raw material received at each plant, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: u
vars.doc: domestic purchases of each raw material at each plant, in thousand tons per year

vars.name: psi
vars.doc: the discounted total cost, the quantity minimized

vars.name: psip
vars.doc: the domestic recurrent cost component, in thousand pounds per year

vars.name: psil
vars.doc: the transport cost component, in thousand pounds per year

vars.name: psii
vars.doc: the import cost component, in thousand pounds per year

query: assiout makes plenty of its own SSP-155, but the plan dribbles tiny token amounts to some of its natural upper-Egypt markets while trucking elsewhere. Shouldn't assiout serve a solid baseline — at least 30 of SSP-155 into every one of its upper-Egypt outlets, minia, assiout town, sohag, quena and aswan alike? What's keeping the model from giving each of those southern markets that floor from assiout's own plant?
expected_pyomo:
def _assiout_floor(model, j): return model.xf['ssp-155', 'assiout', j] >= 30.0
model.assiout_ue_service = Constraint(['minia', 'assiout', 'sohag', 'quena', 'aswan'], rule=_assiout_floor)

query: Two things about the superphosphate plan bug me together: kafr-el-zt is pinned right at its ceiling on SSP-155 while assiout barely keeps any of its own output for the assiout home market. Shouldn't we ease kafr-el-zt back to under 140 and at the same time make assiout cover at least 80 of its own local SSP-155 demand from its own plant? What's the model seeing that makes it lean on kafr-el-zt and ship assiout's output away instead?
